coredata.dc:title: Evaluation of chemotherapeutic response in living cells using subcellular Organelle‒Selective amphipathic carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Ren-Siang
Lin, Yu-Syuan
Nain, Amit
Unnikrishnan, Binesh
Lin, Yu-Feng
Yang, Cheng-Ruei
Chen, Tzu-Heng
Huang, Yu-Fen
Huang, Chih-Ching
Chang, Huan-Tsung
coredata.dc:description: Monitoring of structural changes in subcellular organelles is critical to evaluate the chemotherapeutic response of cells. However, commercial organelle selective fluorophores are easily photobleached, and thus are unsuitable for real-time and long-term observation. We have developed photostable carbon-dot liposomes (CDsomes)-based fluorophores for organellar and suborganellar imaging to circumvent these issues. The CDs synthesized through a mild pyrolysis/hydrolysis process exhibit amphipathic nature and underwent self-assembly to form liposome–like structures (CDsomes). The controlled hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity-guided preparation of CDsomes are used to selectively and rapidly (<1 min) stain nucleolus, cytoplasm, and membrane. In addition, the CDsomes offer universal high-contrast staining not only in fixed cells but also in living cells, allowing real-time observation and morphological identification in the specimen. The as-prepared CDsomes exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence, and are much more stable under photoirradiation (e.g., ultraviolet light) than traditional subcellular dyes. Interestingly, the CDsomes can be transferred to daughter cells by diluting the particles, enabling multigenerational tracking of suborganelle for up to six generations, without interrupting the staining pattern. Therefore, we believe that the ultra-photostable CDsomes with high biocompatibility, and long-term suborganellar imaging capabilities, hold a great potential for screening and evaluating therapeutic performance of various chemotherapeutic drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Liposomes
Suborganellar imaging
Photostability
Long-term tracking
Chemotherapeutic responses

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluating the long-term staining of CDsomes and CDs in HeLa cells
CDsomesmem probing membrane potential changes induced by CCCP
CDsomesnuc probing nucleolar morphological changes induced by actinomycin D
Ultrafast, excellent photostability, versatile and comprehensive staining characteristics

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM images, (B) UV–Vis absorption spectra, and (C) fluorescence spectra of 100 μg mL−1 of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem prepared in 10 mM sodium phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4). Insert to A: Magnification of representative TEM images of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem (from left to right). The fluorescence intensities (IF) were plotted in arbitrary units.

Multi-color subcellular imaging of NIH-3T3, HeLa, and MCF-7 cells with treatment using (A) CDsomesnuc (200 μg mL−1), (B) CDscyto (200 μg mL−1), and (C) CDsomesmem (200 μg mL−1) in sodium phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) for 10 min (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity). Cell images in blue (λex = 360–370; λem ≥ 420 nm), green (λex = 460–490 nm; λem ≥ 520 nm), and red (λex = 525–545 nm; λem ≥ 575 nm) fluorescence channels were collected using an inverted fluorescence microscope. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Photostability evaluation of (A) CDsomesnuc‒ and (B) CDscyto‒stained HeLa cells, and the corresponding fluorescence intensity profiles along the white line in (C,D) blue, (E,F) green, and (G,H) red fluorescence channel. The cells were co-stained with Hoechst 33,342 (0.5 μg mL−1) and CDsomesnuc (200 μg mL−1, in A, C, E, and F) or CDscyto (200 μg mL−1, B, D, F, and H), and exposed to continuous UV-light irradiation (λex = 360–370) for 10 min. Cell images in blue fluorescence channel (λex = 360–370; λem ≥ 420 nm) were collected (a) before and (b) after UV-light irradiation to demonstrate the superior photostability of CDsomes over organic fluorophore. Fluorescence images in green (λex = 460–490 nm; λem ≥ 520 nm) and red (λex = 525–545 nm; λem ≥ 575 nm) fluorescence channels were also collected, which showed negligible changes in the fluorescence of CDsomesnuc and CDscyto even after UV-light irradiation. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Monitoring of CCCP-induced membrane potential changes in HeLa cells using CDsomesmem. (A) Fluorescence images showed CCCP-induced subcellular redistribution of the CDsomesmem and (B) fluorescence intensities of the CDsomesmem-labeled cells responded to CCCP. The cells were labeled with 50 μg mL−1 of CDsomesmem in PBS for 30 min, and then treated with PBS containing 0–400 μM of CCCP for 30 min. Fluorescence increments were measured at 460 nm under excitation at 360 nm using a microplate reader. The error bars represent standard deviations from three repeated measurements. Cell images in a bright field and blue fluorescence channel (λex = 360–370; λem ≥ 420 nm) were collected using an inverted fluorescence microscope. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Bright-field and fluorescence images of shrinkage and disappearance of the nucleoli induced by a chemotherapeutic drug (actinomycin D). HeLa cells without treatment or treated with DMEM containing various concentrations of actinomycin D for 2 h and then fixed with pre-chilled 100% methanol for 30 min at −20 °C, followed by labeling with CDsomesnuc (0.2 mg mL−1) in PBS for 10 min at ambient temperature to observe the changes in nucleolar integrity caused by actinomycin D. Cell images in bright field, blue (λex = 360–370; λem ≥ 420 nm), green (λex = 460–490 nm; λem ≥ 520 nm), and red (λex = 525–545 nm; λem ≥ 575 nm) fluorescence channels were collected using an inverted fluorescence microscope. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Graphical representation of (A) nucleolus-targeting CDsomes (CDsomesnuc), (B) cytoplasm-targeting carbon dots (CDscyto), and (C) membrane-targeting CDsomes (CDsomesmem) and their applications in subcellular imaging.

head.ce:title: Evaluation of chemotherapeutic response in living cells using subcellular Organelle‒Selective amphipathic carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical and Environmental Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, 1, Section 4, Roosevelt Road, Taipei, 10617, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Monitoring of structural changes in subcellular organelles is critical to evaluate the chemotherapeutic response of cells. However, commercial organelle selective fluorophores are easily photobleached, and thus are unsuitable for real-time and long-term observation. We have developed photostable carbon-dot liposomes (CDsomes)-based fluorophores for organellar and suborganellar imaging to circumvent these issues. The CDs synthesized through a mild pyrolysis/hydrolysis process exhibit amphipathic nature and underwent self-assembly to form liposome–like structures (CDsomes). The controlled hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity-guided preparation of CDsomes are used to selectively and rapidly (<1 min) stain nucleolus, cytoplasm, and membrane. In addition, the CDsomes offer universal high-contrast staining not only in fixed cells but also in living cells, allowing real-time observation and morphological identification in the specimen. The as-prepared CDsomes exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence, and are much more stable under photoirradiation (e.g., ultraviolet light) than traditional subcellular dyes. Interestingly, the CDsomes can be transferred to daughter cells by diluting the particles, enabling multigenerational tracking of suborganelle for up to six generations, without interrupting the staining pattern. Therefore, we believe that the ultra-photostable CDsomes with high biocompatibility, and long-term suborganellar imaging capabilities, hold a great potential for screening and evaluating therapeutic performance of various chemotherapeutic drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional fluorophores are widely used in cell biology for the labeling and identification of particular cellular organelles, such as plasma membrane, cytoplasm, lysosome, mitochondria, Golgi apparatus, endosome, nuclear membrane, nucleus, and nucleolus (Liu et al., 2020; Maria et al., 2014; Czaplińska et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2020; Wallabregue et al., 2016; Taniyama et al., 2018; Suseela et al., 2018; Cao et al., 2019). Monitoring of structural changes in the subcellular levels is vital in understanding cellular behaviors under various external and/or internal stimuli. For instance, rupture/integrity of plasma membrane determines the fate of programmed cell death‒necrosis or apoptosis (Zhang et al., 2018). Similarly, morphological changes in nucleoli indicate the abnormal cell proliferation during transformation (Weeks et al., 2019). Nucleoli are suborganelles inside nuclei, which are responsible for producing ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and play a key role in protein synthesis (Abraham et al., 2020). In general, the quantities and sizes of nucleoli in cells increase and enlarge, respectively, during malignant transformation (Penzo et al., 2019). Thus, continuous assessment of subcellular organelles is necessary to understand how cells respond to different manipulations such as pharmacological treatment.

Over the years, organic fluorophores, such as Lyso Tracker, lipophilic tracers, and Hoechst, have been employed for fluorescence-based cell labeling via complex mechanisms, including protonation-assisted retention, membrane fusion, and DNA minor groove binding (Yapici et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2020; Bucevičius et al., 2018). However, organic dyes are prone to photobleaching, which restricts the possibility of live cell imaging to observe structural changes. Alternatively, numerous nanomaterials based on silica, metals, and semiconductors have been developed, which manifest excellent photostability relative to organic fluorophores. Still, the demand of targeting ligands, the associated low specificity and high in vitro cytotoxicity limited their usage (Burns et al., 2006; Zhang and Wang, 2014; Nikazar et al., 2020).

On the other hand, carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, such as carbon dots (CDs), have been extensively studied for analytical, bioimaging, drug delivery, therapeutics, antibacterial applications, etc., due to their superior biocompatibility, wide visible light spectrum imaging, and negligible photobleaching relative to other nanomaterials and conventional organic fluorophores (Xu et al., 2019; Chang, 2021; Chu et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2020; Unnikrishnan et al., 2020; Ross et al., 2020; Anand et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2019). For example, Chang et al. have prepared glycine-derived CDs through a simple one-pot hydrothermal approach for imaging of MCF-10A and MCF-7 cells (Hsu and Chang, 2012). The as-prepared glycine-CDs exhibit excellent photostability and negligible cytotoxicity against tested cell lines with a concentration up to 1.72 mg mL−1, which, however, are incapable of targeted staining. Kang's group achieved organelle-selective labeling of nucleolus via ascorbic acid-derived CDs, mainly attributed to their specific interaction with RNA and DNA (Li et al., 2018). Although a few organelle or suborganelle-selective CDs have demonstrated targeting specificity, they have not been used to screen and evaluate the morphological changes in subcellular organelles in response to chemotherapeutic treatment (Xu et al., 2019; Ali et al., 2020; Li et al., 2017, 2020; Geng et al., 2019). Thus, a systematic study of the corresponding behavior of CDs is essential to unravel the effects of therapeutic drugs on cells and subcellular organelles.

In this work, we synthesized triglyceride-transformed CDs through pyrolysis. The as-formed CDs after hydrolysis were extracted in butanol to collect the hydrophobic portion in the organic phase, while the hydrophilic part remains in the aqueous phase. The separated CDs in the organic phase were partially inherited with several hydrophobic chains of triglyceride, with their structural orientation similar to that of phospholipids. Therefore, the amphiphilic CDs undergo self-assembly to form liposome-like structures, named CDsomes. The controlled hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity-guided preparation of different types of CDsomes are used to selectively and rapidly (<1 min) stain nucleolus, cytoplasm, and membrane (Scheme 1 ). High contrast images of CDsomes–labeled nucleolus, cytoplasm, and membrane in live or fixed cells with minimal non-targeted distribution were promoted by several unique properties of CDsomes, including assembly/disassembly, various sizes (CDsomes <100 nm and CDs <5 nm), abundance of hydroxyl or carboxyl groups, and intrinsic lipophilic properties. We discovered that the highly photostable CDsomes could be transferred to the daughter cells, featuring multigenerational tracking of the organelle or suborganelle for up to 6 generations without any interference in the staining pattern. More importantly, membrane potential changes under the influence of carbonyl cyanide 3-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP) were directly correlated with the fluorescence intensity of membrane-targeting CDsomes. In addition, morphological changes in nucleolus under the influence of actinomycin D were probed by observing the fluorescence patterns of nucleolus-targeting CDsomes. Furthermore, both CDs and CDsomes showed negligible cytotoxicity up to the 300 μg mL−1, revealing their superior biocompatibility, thus holding a great potential for screening and evaluating long-term chemotherapeutic responses at suborganelle level.

Hoechst 33,342, DAPI (4', 6-diamidino-2-phenylindole), Alexa Fluor 594 phalloidin, and alamarBlue reagent were obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Waltham, MA, USA). MCF-7 (BCRC 60436), NIH-3T3 (BCRC 60008), and HeLa (BCRC 60005) cell lines were procured from the Bioresource Collection and Research Center (BCRC, Hsinchu, Taiwan). RPMI-1640 medium, Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), non-essential amino acids (NEAA), and l-glutamine were purchased from Gibco (Grand Island, NY, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from Invitrogen (Eugene, OR, USA). Paraformaldehyde (PFA), Triton X-100, ethanol, carbonyl cyanide 3-chlorophenylhydrazone (CCCP), actinomycin D, and ribonuclease A (RNase) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). l-α-phosphatidylserine (brain, porcine) (sodium salt) was bought from Avanti Polar Lipids, Inc. (Industrial Park Drive Alabaster, Alabama, USA). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS; containing 137 mM NaCl, 5.0 mM KCl, 10 mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0 mM KH2PO4; pH 7.4) was used to mimic physiological conditions. Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2 MN cm; EMD Millipore, Billerica, MA, USA) was used in all experiments.

Briefly, 30 g of triolein was heated at 220 °C in Teflon bottle for 3 days. Post pyrolysis, the crude product was dispersed in sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and sonicated for 2 h. The CDsomesnuc were obtained after dialysis (MWCO = 15 kD) against PBS, with surrounding buffer being changed every 1 h. We employed liquid-liquid extraction in butanol/water to separate CDscyto and CDsomesmem from the CDsomesnuc. Solutions containing CDscyto and CDsomesmem were extracted in aqueous phase and organic phase, respectively and were individually purified through dialysis against PBS (same as above). In addition, the batch stability of the CDsomes and CDs in terms of production yield, quantum yield (QY), size distribution, labeling capacities, and storage conditions were also investigated.

The particle sizes and morphologies of the CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem were obtained using a Tecnai 20 G2 S-Twin transmission electron microscope (Philips/FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). Fluorescence and UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDsomes solutions were recorded using a monochromatic microplate spectrophotometer (Synergy 4 Multi-Mode; Biotek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA). The fluorescence QYs of the CDs and CDsomes were determined by using quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as a reference. Shortly, the absorbance and the corresponding fluorescence spectra of the CDsomes, CDs, and quinine sulfate at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm were recorded respectively. Five different concentrations of CDsomes, CDs, and quinine sulfate solutions were prepared for QY measurement. A plot of integrated fluorescence versus absorbance was drawn, from which a slope M was obtained and used to calculate the QY according to the following equation:
φ x
=
φ s
(
M x
M s
)
(
η x
η s
)
2

where the subscripts s and x denote the standard (quinine sulfate) and test samples (CDsomes/CDs), respectively, and φ is QY, and η is the refractive index of the water solvent (equal to 1.33). The fluorescence lifetime of the CDs and CDsomes was recorded using a photo-counting PicoHarp 300 system (Pico PicoQuant, Berlin, Germany) with a diode laser emitting at 375 nm (FluoTime 300) as the light source. The hydrodynamic sizes of the CDs and CDsomes were assessed using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK).

NIH-3T3 (mouse embryonic fibroblast) and HeLa (human cervical adenocarcinoma) cells were maintained in DMEM medium with high glucose (4.5 g L−1), while MCF-7 cells (human breast adenocarcinoma) were maintained in RPMI medium. Both media were supplemented with 10% (v/v) FBS, 1% NEAA, 1% l-glutamine, penicillin (100 IU mL−1), and streptomycin (100 mg mL−1). All cell lines were cultured in a humidified atmosphere at 37 °C with 95% air and 5% CO2.

MCF-7, NIH-3T3, and HeLa cells (1 × 105 cells) were seeded on 10-mm coverslips placed in 24-well plates. After overnight incubation (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity), the cells were washed once with PBS, and treated with CDs or CDsomes (200 μg mL−1; in PBS) for 10 min at 37 °C, with 5% CO2, and 95% humidity. The cells were then washed once with PBS, fixed with 4% PFA for 15 min at 37 °C, and mounted on glass slides with a hardening mounting medium (Sigma-Aldrich). The cells were examined for the intracellular distribution of CDs or CDsomes in different fluorescence channels under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus BX 51, Tokyo, Japan) or a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Leica, Heidelberg, Germany). The cell nuclei and filamentous actin (F-actin) were respectively stained with DAPI and Alexa Fluor 594 dye according to the manufacturer's instructions (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA).

HeLa cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a concentration of 8 × 103 per well in 200 μL of culture medium and allowed to acclimate for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity). Subsequently, the medium in the wells was replaced with PBS containing various concentrations (5, 50, 100, 200, and 300 μg mL−1) of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, or CDsomesmem. After 10 min of incubation (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity) with CDs or CDsomes, the cells were carefully washed once with PBS, and incubated for an additional 48 h in culture medium (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity) prior to cell viability testing. After 48 h of incubation, the cells were washed once with PBS, and treated with the alamarBlue reagent (10-fold dilution in culture medium, 200 μL per well) for 2 h. Then, the fluorescence intensity of alamarBlue-stained cells was measured at 590 nm under excitation wavelength of 560 nm using a fluorescence microplate reader (Synergy 4). Each condition was repeated three times. Because the fluorescence intensity is directly correlated with the cell quantity, cell viability was calculated by assuming 100% viability in the control set (without CDs or CDsomes treatments).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluating the long-term staining of CDsomes and CDs in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: The stabilities of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem staining were evaluated by examining their fluorescence intensities and distributions in cells at different periods of incubation time intervals from 1 to 6 days. HeLa cells were plated at a density of 1 × 104 per well on 10-mm coverslips placed in 24-well plates. After overnight incubation (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity), the cells were washed once with PBS, and treated with 200 μg mL−1 of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, or CDsomesmem in PBS for 10 min (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity). The cells were then washed once with PBS, and incubated in culture medium for different periods of 1–6 days. At each time point, cells were washed once with PBS, fixed with 4% PFA for 15 min at 37 °C, and mounted on glass slides with a hardening mounting medium. The fluorescence intensity and the distribution of CDs or CDsomes in cells were examined at different fluorescence channels under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus BX 51).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDsomesmem probing membrane potential changes induced by CCCP
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were plated at a density of 1 × 105 per well on 10-mm coverslips placed in 24-well plates. After overnight incubation (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity), cells were washed once with PBS, and treated with PBS containing 50 μg mL−1 of CDsomesmem for 30 min (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% relative humidity). Subsequently, the CDsomesmem solutions were replaced with PBS containing various concentrations of CCCP (25–400 μM) for 30 min (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity). At last, the cells were washed once with PBS, fixed with 4% PFA for 15 min at 37 °C, and mounted on glass slides with a hardening mounting medium. The membrane potential changes induced by CCCP were examined by observing the fluorescence intensity and intracellular distribution of CDsomesmem in blue fluorescence channel (excitation wavelength: 360–370 nm; emission wavelength: ≥ 420 nm) under an inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus BX 51). CCCP-induced membrane potential changes were also quantified by measuring fluorescence at 460 under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm using a fluorescence microplate reader (Synergy 4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDsomesnuc probing nucleolar morphological changes induced by actinomycin D
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were plated at a density of 1 × 105 per well on 10-mm coverslips placed in 24-well plates. After overnight incubation (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity), cells were washed once with PBS, and treated with DMEM containing various concentrations of actinomycin D (0.012–50 μM) for 2 h (37 °C, 5% CO2, and 95% humidity). Subsequently, the cells were washed once with PBS, fixed with pre-chilled 100% methanol for 30 min at −20 °C, and labeled with CDsomesnuc (0.2 mg mL−1) in PBS for 10 min at room temperature. At last, the cells were washed twice with PBS, and mounted on glass slides with hardening mounting medium. Actinomycin D–induced nucleolar morphological changes were examined by observing the staining pattern of CDsomesnuc in the nucleoli in different fluorescence channels under an inverted fluorescence microscope.

Two types of CDs were obtained through pyrolysis of triglyceride and followed by hydrolysis and purification slightly modified from our previous report (Chen and Chang, 2017; Chang and Chen, 2019; Chang et al., 2020). Afterwards, the lipophilic CDs (i.e., CDs decorated with asymmetric hydrophobic chains) were extracted by butanol. Whereas the hydrophilic CDs (i.e., CDscyto; Scheme 1B) remained in the aqueous solution. After the lyophilization of the lipophilic CDs and resuspended in aqueous solution, the self-assembled CDsomesmem (i.e., lipophilic CDs-constructed liposome-like structures) were obtained (Scheme 1C). The lyophilized lipophilic CDs resuspended in the hydrophilic CDs solution allow forming CDsomesnuc (Scheme 1A), consisting of CDsomesmem embedded with hydrophilic CDs. The lipophilic CDs are partially decorated with hydrophobic chains of the precursors. The structural orientation of as-prepared CDs is similar to that of phospholipids, therefore, they undergo self-assembly to form nanosome structures, therefore named as CDsomes. The controlled hydrophilicity or hydrophobicity-guided preparation of CDsomes and CDs were then used to selectively and rapidly stain subcellular organelles. Representative transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDsomesnuc and CDsomesmem are depicted in Fig. 1 A with mean diameters of 89 and 85 nm, respectively, while CDscyto showed a uniform distribution of tiny sized (∼4 nm) CDs. Magnification of TEM images of CDsomesnuc and CDsomesmem showed liposome structures consisting of many small CDs (inserts to Fig. 1A). It is worth mentioning that results from our previous report have evidenced the existence of several tens of thousands of small CDscyto on the surface of the CDsomesnuc (Chen and Chang, 2017; Chang and Chen, 2019; Chang et al., 2020). The amphiphilic interaction among the surface oleate ligands of the CDscyto was inferred as the driving force for the recruitment of CDscyto on the CDsomesnuc (Chen and Chang, 2017; Chang and Chen, 2019; Chang et al., 2020). The hydrodynamic diameters of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) are 98.8 ± 18.3 nm (PDI = 0.4), 10.7 ± 1.4 nm (PDI = 0.3), and 92.0 ± 13.6 nm (PDI = 0.4), which corroborates with that obtained from the TEM measurements (Fig. S1). The zeta potentials of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem were determined to be ca. −74, −85, and −39 mV, respectively. The electrostatic repulsion between individual particles prevents them from self-aggregation, thereby contributing to their superior aqueous dispersibility and colloidal stability.

Each of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem shows an obvious peak at 230 nm, ascribed to the π→π* transitions of conjugated CC bonds and a shoulder band at 250–350 nm that is attributed to the n→π* transitions of CO bonds (Fig. 1B) (Jian et al., 2017). The fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem determined using quinine sulfate (QY; 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) as reference are 4.12%, 4.56%, and 1.02%, respectively. Each of the CDs exhibits an emission maximum at the wavelength of ca. 480 nm at a fixed excitation wavelength of 400 nm (Fig. 1C). Obvious excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emissions in CDsomes or CDs were mainly observed due to the formation of different sizes of polycyclic aromatic clusters, surface emissive traps, edge defects, as well as various sizes of CDs (Fig. S2) (Liu et al., 2019; Yan et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2020; Miao et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2017). The fluorescence decay profiles of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem show two fluorescence lifetime components, 7.4 (56.7%)/1.8 (43.3%), 7.1 (74.7%)/1.2 (25.4%), and 8.5 (43.5%)/1.9 (56.5%) ns, respectively, ascribing to diverse energy levels present in the CDs. (Fig. S3) (Nie et al., 2014). It is worth noting that the batch-to-batch variation in the formulation yield, size, and fluorescence QY of CDsomes prepared by the proposed method is less than 11% (data not shown), which is significantly lower than those reporeted for conventional liposome formulations (>20%) (Berger et al., 2001). In addition, CDsomes are highly robust due to their long-term colloidal and optical stability; the efficiency of intracellular labeling remains unaffected even when stored at room temperature for 1 year.
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2022.114362

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

Compatibility of the CDs and CDsomes with conventional organic fluorophores, such as Hoechst 33,342 (excitation/emission wavelengths = 350/461 nm), and Alexa Fluor 594 phalloidin (excitation/emission wavelengths = 581/609 nm) was investigated. The former is a cell-permeable double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) binding dye, whereas the latter is a cell-impermeable cyclic peptide conjugated fluorescent dye that holds a strong binding affinity towards filamentous actin (F-actin) (Bucevičius et al., 2018; Faulstich et al., 1988). Fig. S6 elucidates the fluorescence images of HeLa cells after co-staining, which showed a confined blue fluorescence in the nucleus due to Hoechst 33,342 (Fig. S6B), and a green staining pattern in the nucleoli, cytoplasm or membrane due to CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem, respectively. The observation of Alexa Fluor 594 phalloidin-guided F-actin labeling in the cytoplasm recorded in the red fluorescence channel also revealed the stability of CDsomes and CDs in specific locations of the cells even after Triton X-100 permeabilization, which was necessary for F-actin staining. Our results suggested that CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem offer specific fluorescence tagging to subcellular level and are compatible with common commercial staining reagents and permeabilization steps. It can lead to interfering-free staining pattern even in the presence of various conventional cellular imaging agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrafast, excellent photostability, versatile and comprehensive staining characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: Along with superior biocompatibility and suborganelle-targeted fluorescent labeling properties, we also investigated the cellular uptake kinetics of CDs. Internalization of CDs was studied by continuously monitoring the fluorescence intensities and staining patterns for 300 s in HeLa cells after being incubated with 200 μg mL−1 of CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, or CDsomesmem. In Fig. S7A, weak fluorescence in the nucleoli was observed soon after 30 s of incubation with CDsomesnuc, which further reached a plateau after 60 s. We noted that upon saturation, excess CDsomesnuc were slightly distributed in nuclei after a longer incubation time (>60 s). Similarly, the CDscyto can rapidly enter the cells and reached stable cytoplasmic distribution within 60 s of incubation (Fig. S7B). In addition, CDsomesmem also showed an instant fusion with plasma and nuclear membranes (<30 s), but a high concentration of CDsomesmem resulted in small aggregates, attributed to the lipophilic properties of CDsomesmem (Fig. S7C). More homogeneous distribution of CDsomesmem in HeLa cells was achieved after incubating with 50 μg mL−1 of CDsomesmem for 30 min (data not shown). These CDs exhibit faster uptake kinetics than most of the reported CDs and commercial fluorophores, probably due to their amphiphilic properties (E et al., 2021; Estandarte et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017). Moreover, most CDs penetrate the cell membrane by endocytosis, which is an energy-dependent process, limiting the imaging of dead or fixed cells. The CDsomesnuc, CDscyto, and CDsomesmem have been separately applied to label HeLa cells prefixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (PFA), and the corresponding imaging capabilities have been successfully demonstrated in Fig. S8. Unlike CDscyto and CDsomesmem, cell permeabilization with Triton X-100 is required for CDsomesnuc to stain PFA-fixed HeLa cells to obtain staining patterns similar to that of live cells. The penetration and distribution of CDsomes and CDs in fixed cells suggested an energy-independent process different from endocytosis pathways, a commonly occurring phenomenon for the cellular internalization of various reported CDs, quantum dots, or other nanoparticles (E et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2014; Donahue et al., 2019; Rees et al., 2019). However, nucleoli cannot be stained with CDsomesnuc in the absence of Triton X-100, implying that nuclear membrane acts as a barrier against the nuclear import (Feldherr and Feldherr, 1960). Thus, CDsomesnuc need nuclear membrane permeabilization to enter fixed cells.

Most organic dyes are significantly susceptible to photobleaching due to the irreversible photochemical alteration of a fluorophore in the structure (Demchenko, 2020). Therefore photostability plays a vital role in the long-term imaging of cells (Unnikrishnan et al., 2020; Ross et al., 2020; Han et al., 2019). Several antifade mountants have been developed to prevent photobleaching during imaging of live and fixed cells, which, however, requires a series of complex steps, and such protection is always temporary (Bogdanov et al., 2012; Cordes et al., 2011). Therefore, the photostability of CDsomes or CDs for cell imaging was evaluated on HeLa cells co-stained with Hoechst 33,342 under UV-light irradiation in the microscopy. It was noted that UV exposure (1 min) resulted in a significant (>50%) loss in blue fluorescence of Hoechst in nuclei, while negligible change in fluorescence intensity was observed in CDsomesnuc located in nucleoli or in cytoplasm labeled with CDscyto. After a longer continuous UV irradiation (10 min) on cells co-stained with CDsomesnuc/Hoechst or CDscyto/Hoechst (Fig. 3 A and B), blue fluorescence images captured with UV light showed that the fluorescence intensity of Hoechst was reduced by 73% and 98%, respectively, while the fluorescence intensity of CDsomesnuc and CDscyto was reduced by 19% and 33% (Fig. 3C and D). The intensity profiles of CDsomesnuc and CDscyto remained unchanged (<1%) across the lines in the green (Fig. 3E and F) and red (Fig. 3G and H) fluorescence images captured by blue and green light, respectively. This finding indicates the superior photostability of CDsomesnuc and CDscyto than Hoechst; the blue fluorescence of CDsomesnuc and CDscyto diminished gradually until reaching a plateau (approximately ∼24% and ∼39% decrease) at 20 min of UV‒light irradiation (data not shown). The fluorophores of organic dyes possess various nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups, which under light irradiation, undergo inevitable structural changes (Demchenko, 2020). In contrast, CDs are much more resistant to photobleaching, which can be attributed to their chemically inert light-emitting aromatic centers, preventing them from photochemical reactions and structural damage (Xu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2019). As a family of CDs, the CDsomesnuc and CDscyto emit multi-color fluorescence mainly originating from the rigid sp2 carbon clusters of varying sizes in their core, which also demonstrate excellent photostability under light irradiation. However, the photostability of CDsomesmem is relatively poor compared to CDsomesnuc and CDscyto. The blue fluorescence of CDsomesmem decreased by 72% after only 1 min of UV-light excitation (Fig. S9), whereas the green and red fluorescence of the CDsomesmem were not affected. The poor photostability of CDsomesmem can be attributed to the fact that the incompletely carbonized polymeric structures were prone to photochemical oxidation under UV-light irradiation (Xiong et al., 2017; Longo et al., 2020).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2022.114362

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 3 Multimedia component 3

Long-term analysis of intracellular movement and location of subcellular organelles are extremely vital to understand dynamic behaviors within cells. However, conventional organic dyes or nanomaterial-based fluorophores tend to leak out of the cells over a period of time (few hours to one day) due to their cellular non-specificity (Zhang et al., 2017; Jiang et al., 2010). We observed that the HeLa cells incubated with CDsomesnuc, CDscyto or CDsomesmem retained the fluorescence for several days (Fig. S13). The CDsomesnuc (Fig. S13A) and CDscyto (Fig. S13B) displayed unaltered fluorescence until day 4, whereas shows a slightly weaker fluorescence intensity on day 6. Besides, CDsomesmem lasted for 6 days on the cells, which retained much longer than other reported fluorophores (Fig. S13C) (Sankaran and Wohland, 2020). The abilities of the CDsomes/CDs to retain in the cells for several days make them versatile tools to track the short- and long-term cell motility and migration. Chemical inertness of CDs allows as-prepared CDsomes and CDscyto to withstand a complex biological environment comprised of bulky proteins, degradative enzymes, and high salt concentrations (Xu et al., 2019; Qu et al., 2020). Our CDs exhibited excellent stability in suborganelles of the cells and were readily transferred from one generation to the next. Owing to their unique amphipathic nature, CDs can be retained in specific suborganelles even after several generations (6 days). Contrary to that, organic fluorophores are susceptible to biochemical environments within cellular compartments and are therefore vulnerable to structural damage or elimination.[56] Moreover, efflux pumps on the plasma membrane eject certain organic fluorophores, making long-term and generational tracking of the suborganelles highly challenging (Jensen, 2012).

Owing to the excellent biocompatibility and photostability, we further elucidated the potential of CDsomes in evaluating the real-time morphological changes and therapeutic responses from CCCP and actinomycin D in living HeLa cells. CCCP induces the changes in the potential of plasma and nuclear membranes, where CDsomesmem were employed as an indicator to measure the changes in membrane potential. CCCP is known to cause uncoupling of the proton gradient established across the membranes by the electron transport chains, which further reduces Na+/K+–ATPase and Na+/Ca2+ exchanger activity of the membrane, thus disrupting the potential across membranes (Park et al., 2002). The response of CCCP at different concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μM) was demonstrated on CDsomesmem‒stained HeLa cells after 30 min of incubation at ambient temperature. Bright-field and fluorescence microscopy images revealed that the cellular distribution of the CDsomesmem was initially confined to the plasma membrane and nuclear membrane. However, it gradually leaked into the cytoplasm and nucleoli after CCCP treatment (Fig. 4 A). Untreated cells showed an intact cell membrane, whereas fluorescence intensity increased with increasing in CCCP concentrations, confirming that the permeabilization of membranes led to the redistribution of CDsomesmem in the cells (Fig. 4B). It is noteworthy that the phenomenon of correlating the membrane potential with quantifiable fluorescence intensity of CDsomesmem holds an excellent scope for sensing the alteration in the membrane microstructures.

Actinomycin D is another chemotherapeutic drug commonly used to treat testicular and ovarian cancer by intercalating with guanine-cytosine (GC) rich regions of rRNA gene rDNA promoter to stabilize type I topoisomerase-rDNA covalent complexes (Kang and Park, 2009). The susceptibility of the nucleoli to actinomycin D is mainly due to its ability to inhibit rRNA transcription in the nucleoli to induce rapid shrinkage and fragmentation of the nucleoli (Olausson et al., 2012). The HeLa cells treated with various concentrations (0.012, 6.25, 25.0, and 50 μM) of actinomycin D show morphological changes in the nucleoli by monitoring the staining patterns of internalized CDsomesnuc (Fig. 5 ). High doses of actinomycin D (6.25, 25.0, and 50 μM) led to fragmentation, shrinkage, and even disappearance of the nucleoli. Our results showed that progressive events in the nucleolar morphologies of actinomycin D-treated cells could be directly monitored by labeling the cells with nucleolus-selective CDsomesnuc. Therefore, we believe that our assay is convenient for evaluating the therapeutic performance of various drugs in different types of cells. Although the cell-permeant SYTO RNASelect Green Fluorescent Cell Stain is currently being utilized for the imaging of the nucleoli in the living cell, poor photostability and lower retention time have limited its application in long-term observation of subcellular organelle (Lu et al., 2015). The application of CDsomesnuc in living cell imaging further circumvents the limitations of traditional immunohistochemistry (IHC) staining of the nucleoli, which usually requires the use of expensive reagents, such as antibodies, chromogens, and multiple steps, including fixation and blocking.

This work demonstrates the ultra-photostable CDs-based fluorophores for suborganellar imaging to show structural changes in subcellular organelles, which is critical to evaluate the chemotherapeutic responses. The CDs synthesized through the pyrolysis/hydrolysis process exhibit amphipathic nature and underwent self-assembly to form liposome–like structures (i.e., CDsomes). The controlled hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity–guided preparation of CDsomes is used to selectively and rapidly (<1 min) stain nucleolus, cytoplasm, and membrane, respectively. In addition, the CDsomes offer universal high-contrast staining not only in living cells but also in fixed cells, allowing real-time observation and morphological identification in the specimen. The as-prepared CDsomes exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence emission, and are much more stable under photoirradiation than classical subcellular dyes. Interestingly, the CDsomes can be transferred to daughter cells through dilution, enabling multigenerational tracking of suborganelle for up to six generations without affected staining pattern. Therefore, we believe that photostable CDsomes with superior biocompatibility (up to 300 μg mL−1) and long-term suborganellar imaging capabilities hold a great potential for screening and evaluating therapeutic performance. In addition, it is the first time to demonstrate that subcellular distribution of the CDs and CDsomes in different locations can be achievable by controlling their hydrophilic/hydrophobic properties, which sheds light on the future design on fluorescence subcellular imaging probes.

Ren-Siang Wu: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yu-Syuan Lin: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation. Amit Nain: Writing – review & editing. Binesh Unnikrishnan: Writing – review & editing. Yu-Feng Lin: Investigation, Methodology. Cheng-Ruei Yang: Investigation, Methodology. Tzu-Heng Chen: Investigation, Methodology. Yu-Fen Huang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chih-Ching Huang: Conceptualization, Supervision. Huan-Tsung Chang: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

We thank Ms. Chia-Ying Chien of the Ministry of Science and Technology (National Taiwan University) for assistance with the TEM measurements.
